text:
MRI LIVER WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST (HEPATOBILIARY PHASE)
Gadoxetate Disodium (Eovist/Primovist) Enhanced Protocol

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Male patient, age 58. Known sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D mutant, newly diagnosed Stage IV disease. Recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated four hepatic lesions across both lobes, largest 3.8 cm in segment VI, with the primary sigmoid mass measuring 4.5 cm. Referred for dedicated liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast agent for characterization and surgical planning assessment. Question of additional occult lesions not seen on CT. Evaluation of future liver remnant for potential conversion resection.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphase MRI of the liver was performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner with a liver-specific protocol. Sequences acquired include: axial and coronal T2-weighted imaging with and without fat saturation; diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with b-values of 0, 50, 400, and 800 s/mm2, with corresponding apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) maps; pre-contrast T1-weighted gradient echo (GRE) in-phase and out-of-phase; dynamic gadoxetate disodium (Eovist, Bayer HealthCare) contrast-enhanced sequences including late arterial phase, portal venous phase, and delayed phases at 2 and 5 minutes; and hepatobiliary phase (HBP) imaging acquired at 20 minutes following IV contrast administration. IV gadoxetate disodium 0.025 mmol/kg was administered. Respiratory-triggered sequences were utilized where applicable. No adverse reaction to contrast agent.

COMPARISON:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (obtained approximately one week prior).

FINDINGS:

LIVER — MORPHOLOGY AND BACKGROUND PARENCHYMA:
The liver demonstrates normal morphology with smooth contour and no surface nodularity. There is no hepatic atrophy or hypertrophy to suggest underlying chronic liver disease. The liver signal on in-phase and out-of-phase T1-weighted sequences is symmetric and homogeneous without diffuse or geographic signal dropout to suggest steatosis or iron deposition. Hepatobiliary phase uptake is uniform and robust in the normal liver parenchyma, confirming adequate hepatocellular function. No background cirrhotic changes identified.

HEPATIC LESIONS:
Four discrete hepatic lesions are identified, concordant in number and distribution with the recent CT. No additional occult lesions are identified on any sequence, including the hepatobiliary phase.

1. Segment VI lesion (dominant): Measures 3.8 cm in greatest axial dimension. On T2-weighted imaging, this lesion demonstrates moderately increased T2 signal, slightly heterogeneous. On DWI, there is marked restricted diffusion with high signal on b800 and corresponding ADC map suppression (ADC approximately 0.82 x 10^-3 mm2/s), indicative of high cellularity consistent with viable malignancy. Dynamic contrast enhancement reveals ring-like peripheral arterial phase enhancement with central hypoenhancement; portal venous and delayed phases demonstrate progressive central fill-in and washout appearance relative to surrounding liver on delayed imaging. On the hepatobiliary phase, the lesion is markedly hypointense relative to the avidly enhancing liver parenchyma, consistent with absent hepatocellular function within the lesion — a pattern characteristic of a non-hepatocellular malignancy (i.e., metastatic deposit). No capsule. No hemorrhage or macroscopic fat identified.

2. Segment IVa lesion: Measures 2.1 cm in greatest axial dimension. T2 signal mildly elevated; restricted diffusion confirmed (ADC approximately 0.79 x 10^-3 mm2/s). Arterial phase shows peripheral rim enhancement; portal venous and delayed phases demonstrate washout. Hepatobiliary phase: uniformly hypointense against background liver, consistent with non-uptaking metastatic deposit. Situated in the medial segment of the left lobe, superior aspect, in proximity to the middle hepatic vein, though no direct vascular encasement is identified on these sequences.

3. Segment II lesion: Measures 1.3 cm in greatest axial dimension. T2 mildly hyperintense. Restricted diffusion present (ADC approximately 0.81 x 10^-3 mm2/s). Peripheral arterial enhancement with delayed washout pattern. Hepatobiliary phase: hypointense, consistent with metastatic deposit. Located in the lateral segment of the left lobe posterolaterally.

4. Segment VII lesion: Measures 0.9 cm in greatest axial dimension. This lesion, though small, demonstrates clear restricted diffusion (b800 high signal, ADC approximately 0.85 x 10^-3 mm2/s) and hepatobiliary phase hypointensity, confirming its nature as a non-uptaking deposit consistent with metastasis. On CT it was difficult to fully characterize; MRI confirms it as a true metastatic lesion. Located posteriorly in the right hepatic lobe near the hepatic dome.

ASSESSMENT OF HEPATIC VASCULAR ANATOMY AND RESECTABILITY:
The portal vein, hepatic veins, and hepatic arteries are patent. The right hepatic artery arises from the proper hepatic artery and has a normal course. There is no replaced or accessory hepatic artery anatomy that would significantly alter surgical approach. The main portal vein is patent without tumor thrombus. Hepatic veins (right, middle, left) are patent and drain normally into the inferior vena cava.

Regarding the distribution of disease: Lesions are present in segments II, IVa, VI, and VII, constituting true bilobar disease (involvement of both right and left lobes). The segment IVa lesion is situated in proximity to the hepatic venous confluence. Given this bilobar distribution of four lesions and the need to preserve an adequate future liver remnant (FLR), upfront surgical resection would require sacrifice of hepatic parenchyma beyond what can safely be removed in a single operation. Estimated FLR based on segment volumetry would be insufficient for safe one-stage hepatectomy at this time. A two-stage approach or portal vein embolization strategy could be considered in the future pending treatment response.

BILIARY SYSTEM:
Intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. No biliary obstruction. The gallbladder is present, without cholelithiasis or wall thickening. No pericholecystic fluid.

SPLEEN:
Normal in size and signal (approximately 10.8 cm craniocaudal). No focal splenic lesions. No splenomegaly.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas demonstrates normal signal and morphology throughout the head, body, and tail. The pancreatic duct is not dilated. No focal pancreatic masses or signal abnormalities identified.

KIDNEYS:
Bilateral kidneys are of normal morphology and signal on the sequences obtained. A few simple-appearing renal cortical cysts are seen bilaterally, largest 1.3 cm on the right, with no internal complexity. No solid renal masses.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal masses or nodules.

AORTA AND GREAT VESSELS:
The abdominal aorta and its visualized branches are patent. No lymphadenopathy is identified in the upper abdomen on the hepatic protocol sequences obtained (note: thorough nodal evaluation is better assessed on the dedicated CT CAP).

PELVIS AND LOWER ABDOMEN:
Not the primary focus of this examination and incompletely evaluated on this liver-protocol study. No ascites. The sigmoid region is partially visualized and demonstrates wall thickening concordant with the known primary tumor, though full staging of the pelvis is deferred to the recent CT.

IMPRESSION:

1. Four hepatic metastases confirmed, concordant with the recent CT, distributed across segments II (1.3 cm), IVa (2.1 cm), VI (3.8 cm, dominant), and VII (0.9 cm). All four lesions demonstrate imaging features highly characteristic of metastatic adenocarcinoma: restricted diffusion, rim arterial enhancement with washout kinetics, and uniform hypointensity on hepatobiliary phase reflecting absent hepatocellular uptake.

2. No additional occult hepatic lesions are identified on this examination, including the sensitive hepatobiliary phase. The four lesions identified on CT represent the complete hepatic metastatic burden to the best assessment of this study.

3. The hepatic distribution is bilobar, with involvement of both the right and left lobes. Hepatic vascular anatomy is normal without encasement. Future liver remnant assessment indicates that upfront curative-intent hepatic resection is not feasible at this time; a conversion chemotherapy strategy aimed at reducing hepatic tumor burden, potentially combined with interval portal vein embolization if response is achieved in the right lobe, would be required prior to surgical consideration.

4. Background liver parenchyma is normal without steatosis, fibrosis, or cirrhosis, with intact hepatocellular function as reflected by robust hepatobiliary phase uptake.

5. No perihepatic ascites. No biliary obstruction. Remaining visualized upper abdominal organs are unremarkable.

Attending Radiologist: [SIGNATURE ON FILE]
Division of Abdominal Imaging
[INSTITUTION ON FILE]
